quote_zh: ‘Censured’ is an official word, borrowed from the institutional vocabulary of committees and courts. By claiming that Snowball was not merely overlooked but formally censured, Squealer fabricates a bureaucratic record of disgrace. The passive voice—‘he had been censured’—obscures who did the censuring, lending the claim a ghostly, unchallengeable authority. The animals faintly remember Snowball’s wounds, but the word ‘censured’ seems to belong to a realm of documented fact, and they cannot argue with documents that do not exist. Orwell demonstrates that to rewrite history, you do not need to burn records; you need only to speak the new version in the language of official truth.
ex_en: The censure that Squealer invented for Snowball weighed more heavily on the animals’ minds than the blood they had seen running down his back.
ex_zh: The word ‘censured’ was stamped onto Snowball’s name like a brand, and the scar it left was invisible but impossible to ignore.

def_en: intended to make peace, calm anger, or restore goodwill
def_zh: conciliatory message, conciliatory gesture, conciliatory tone
quote_en: "In addition, four pigeons were sent to Foxwood with a conciliatory message, which it was hoped might re‑establish good relations with Pilkington." – The narrator describing Napoleon’s diplomatic response after Frederick’s banknote forgery (Chapter 8)
quote_zh: ‘Conciliatory’ is the language of diplomacy—soothing, peaceable, designed to win back favour. Napoleon sends a conciliatory message to Pilkington not out of friendship but out of panic; Frederick has cheated him, and he needs an ally. The word masks the raw self‑interest behind the gesture with the appearance of goodwill. The pigeons fly off carrying cooing words, and the reader knows that the message is as hollow as the banknotes that precipitated it. Later, when the pigs sit down to drink with Pilkington, the full arc of ‘conciliation’ becomes clear: it is not animals and humans reconciling but pigs and humans becoming indistinguishable.
ex_en: The conciliatory pigeons flew to Foxwood with olive branches in their beaks, but the branches were cut from the same tree that would one day shade the pigs and the men drinking together.
ex_zh: Conciliatory words fluttered from the pigeons’ wings like snowflakes in summer, melting the moment they touched the ground.

def_en: the passionate expression of grief or sorrow
def_zh: cry of lamentation, great lamentation, wail of lamentation
quote_en: "A cry of lamentation went up." – The narrator describing the animals’ reaction to Squealer’s announcement that Comrade Napoleon is dying (Chapter 8)
quote_zh: ‘Lamentation’ is an ancient, almost biblical word, evoking the communal wailing of a people facing the loss of their king. Orwell deploys it with heavy irony, for Napoleon is not dying; he is suffering from a hangover after drinking the whisky found in the farmhouse cellar. The cry of lamentation is a collective performance of grief that has been stage‑managed by the pigs, and the grandeur of the word only deepens the pathos of the animals’ delusion. The animals have been brought to such a state of emotional dependency that they weep for the Leader who starves them. The word elevates their sorrow into something almost sacred, but the reader sees only a cruel farce.
ex_en: The lamentation for Napoleon’s supposed death was the saddest sound ever heard on the farm, sadder even than the silence that followed the executions.
ex_zh: The lamentation rose into the night like a fog of grief so thick that no one could see the pig in the farmhouse laughing into his whisky.

def_en: to manage to do something by skill, ingenuity, or trickery
def_zh: contrive to escape, contrive a plan, contrive an excuse
quote_en: "A rumour went round that Snowball had after all contrived to introduce poison into Napoleon's food." – The narrator reporting the explanation that circulated for Napoleon’s sudden illness (Chapter 8)
quote_zh: The verb ‘contrived’ presents Snowball as a figure of diabolical resourcefulness, able to reach from exile into the Leader’s very plate. The word suggests a cunning plan successfully executed, and its use keeps Snowball alive as a perpetual, active threat. The rumour is, of course, a fabrication: Napoleon is not poisoned but drunk. Yet the word ‘contrived’ does crucial political work, transforming an embarrassing accident into a tale of enemy malice that reinforces the need for vigilance, guards, and total obedience. The animals swallow the contrived story as readily as Napoleon swallowed the whisky.
ex_en: The rumour that Snowball had contrived to poison Napoleon was a lie that served its purpose: it kept the animals afraid of the wrong pig.
ex_zh: The pigs contrived a story so seamless that not a single drop of truth could leak through; the animals drank it down with their evening mash, and it tasted like safety.

def_en: a soft, moist mass of material applied to the body to relieve soreness or inflammation
def_zh: apply a poultice, herbal poultice, poultice of herbs
quote_en: "Clover treated the hoof with poultices of herbs which she prepared by chewing them" – The narrator describing Clover’s care for Boxer’s split hoof after the Battle of the Windmill (Chapter 9)
quote_zh: Orwell’s ‘poultices’ is a word of tender, homemade medicine. It evokes a pre‑industrial world of folk remedies, where a horse chews herbs into a paste and presses them to a wound with no doctor, no hospital, only love. The image is intensely maternal: Clover becomes a nurse, a mother, a comrade who cannot save Boxer but can try to soothe him. The poultices are organic and temporary, unlike the cold medical bureaucracy that will later send Boxer to the knacker. The word also carries a quiet desperation: the farm has no veterinary resources, so chewed herbs are the only medicine available. The poultices buy Boxer time but cannot mend the deeper hurt of a system that will work him until he collapses.
ex_en: The poultices Clover made with her own breath were the last true kindness Boxer would receive before the van came to take him away.
ex_zh: The poultice lay on Boxer’s hoof like a cool, green whisper of hope—a promise from the earth that the pigs had already broken.

def_en: retired or disqualified from work because of age
def_zh: superannuated animals, superannuated horse, superannuated worker
quote_en: "Now that the small field beyond the orchard had been set aside for barley, it was rumoured that a corner of the large pasture was to be fenced off and turned into a grazing‑ground for superannuated animals." – The narrator relaying the rumour of a retirement pasture that keeps the animals hoping (Chapter 9)
quote_zh: The adjective ‘superannuated’ is a bureaucratic euphemism that weighs down the natural process of growing old with a leaden, institutional formality. Derived from Latin *super* (over) and *annus* (year), it reduces a lifetime of labour to a mere accounting of years. The rumour of a grazing‑ground for the superannuated is a promise that will never be kept, yet it functions as a crucial tool of control: while Boxer dreams of his corner of the pasture, he works himself to death. The word’s polysyllabic length makes it sound official and therefore believable, masking the fact that no animal on the farm has ever actually retired. The superannuated pasture is the farm’s heaven, as distant as Sugarcandy Mountain.
ex_en: Boxer worked towards a superannuated life he would never reach, the promised corner of the pasture receding with every boulder he dragged up the quarry slope.
ex_zh: Superannuated was a word that gleamed like a distant gate at the end of a road, but the road stretched on while the old horse’s legs grew shorter with every step.

def_en: having irregular patches of two colours, typically black and white
def_zh: piebald pig, piebald horse, piebald coat
quote_en: "The young pigs were piebald, and as Napoleon was the only boar on the farm, it was possible to guess at their parentage." – The narrator describing the litter of thirty‑one piglets born to the four sows (Chapter 9)
quote_zh: The word ‘piebald’ is simultaneously descriptive and damning. On the surface, it notes the piglets’ black‑and‑white markings with a neutral, agricultural precision. But because Napoleon is the only boar, the piebald patches become a visible signature of his paternity, a genetic confession that the leader who speaks of equality has been breeding a dynasty. The word’s homely, rustic quality—usually applied to ponies and cart‑horses—attaches to the piglets with a faintly ironic ordinariness, belying the monumental shift in the farm’s structure they represent. The ‘piebald’ litter heralds the arrival of an inherited ruling class, a new generation raised in the farmhouse and educated by Napoleon himself while the other animals watch their own offspring labour.
ex_en: The piebald piglets, black and white like patches of night and snow, were the new generation raised in the farmhouse while the other animals’ children slept in straw.
ex_zh: Piebald as a puzzle of shadow and light, the young pigs trotted through the yard marked by a lineage that promised nothing but more whips.

def_en: performed or occurring as a result of a sudden impulse; unplanned
def_zh: spontaneous demonstration, spontaneous celebration, spontaneous applause
quote_en: "Napoleon had commanded that once a week there should be held something called a Spontaneous Demonstration, the object of which was to celebrate the struggles and triumphs of Animal Farm." – The narrator describing one of Napoleon’s new rituals of enforced celebration (Chapter 9)
quote_zh: The adjective ‘Spontaneous’ is the novel’s most perfectly Orwellian linguistic inversion. A spontaneous event is, by definition, unforced and natural, yet Napoleon commands these demonstrations to happen on a fixed schedule in military formation. The capital letter seals the irony: Spontaneous Demonstration is a proper noun, an institution whose name mocks its own definition. The ritual replaces the genuine, unplanned joy that once erupted after the Rebellion with a performance of loyalty that must be staged every week. The sheep bleat, the cockerel crows, the flag flutters, and the animals can momentarily forget their empty bellies—but the feeling of true communal ownership has been replaced by a script. Spontaneity has been conscripted.
ex_en: The Spontaneous Demonstrations were as unspontaneous as the snow in January, yet the animals marched because to refuse was to be noticed by the dogs.
ex_zh: Spontaneous joy, once as common as hayseed, was now scheduled on a Sunday morning and delivered in a green‑flagged parade that tasted of orders.

def_en: the state of being involved with others in an illegal or wrongful act
def_zh: complicity with Jones, alleged complicity, reveal complicity
quote_en: "On the same day it was given out that fresh documents had been discovered which revealed further details about Snowball's complicity with Jones." – The narrator announcing yet another revision of Snowball’s history after Napoleon becomes President (Chapter 9)
quote_zh: The noun ‘complicity’ adds a layer of legal and moral weight to the accusation against Snowball. It implies not mere treachery but a web of conspiracy, a shared criminal purpose with the very human the Rebellion overthrew. The word’s Latin root (*complicare*, to fold together) suggests two figures intertwined in a hidden pact. The pigs deploy ‘complicity’ as a narrative device: every fresh revelation tightens the story of Snowball as the hidden author of all misfortune, and the documents, which no animal can read, are invoked as irrefutable evidence. The animals’ dim memories of Snowball’s wounds are overwritten by the crushing authority of the word. Complicity becomes a prison made of syllables.
ex_en: The tale of Snowball’s complicity with Jones grew more elaborate with every retelling, a story that had no end because its author was still writing it.
ex_zh: Complicity was a shadow Snowball never cast, but the pigs painted it on the barn wall anyway, using ink made from the animals’ nodding heads.

def_en: characterized by loud, unrestrained noise and laughter
def_zh: uproarious singing, uproarious laughter, uproarious party
quote_en: "That night there was the sound of uproarious singing, which was followed by what sounded like a violent quarrel and ended at about eleven o'clock with a tremendous crash of glass." – The narrator describing the pigs’ drunken banquet in honour of Boxer’s memory (Chapter 9)
quote_zh: ‘Uproarious’ describes the sound of the pigs celebrating with the money they received for Boxer’s body. The word conjures a scene of raucous, abandoned festivity—singing so loud it carries across the yard—and the juxtaposition is devastating. Boxer, who never shirked and never complained, is dead, and his memorial banquet dissolves into a drunken brawl. The ‘uproarious singing’ is a parody of the communal choruses of ‘Beasts of England’ that once united the farm; now the singing comes from the farmhouse, and the animals outside can only listen. The word’s energy mocks the silence of the bereaved, and the crash of glass that ends the night is the sound of every principle shattering.
ex_en: The uproarious singing in the farmhouse that night celebrated a horse the pigs had sold to the knacker, and the animals in the yard stood still, their grief too heavy for laughter.
ex_zh: Uproarious as a storm of empty bottles, the pigs’ voices clinked and shattered while Boxer’s ghost stood silent at the five‑barred gate.

def_en: sullen and ill‑tempered; gloomily irritable
def_zh: morose silence, morose expression, morose donkey
quote_en: "Only old Benjamin was much the same as ever, except for being a little greyer about the muzzle, and, since Boxer's death, more morose and taciturn than ever." – The narrator describing Benjamin’s deepened withdrawal after the loss of his only friend (Chapter 10)
quote_zh: ‘Morose’ adds a dimension of active, brooding bitterness to Benjamin’s established silence. Before Boxer’s death, Benjamin was merely ‘cryptic’; afterwards, he is ‘morose’, a word that suggests a gloom that has soured into something sharper. The donkey’s grief has no outlet—he cannot protest, and his cynicism has been proven right at the cost of his closest companion. The adjective, from the Latin *morosus* (peevish, self‑willed), implies a stubborn, almost aggressive melancholy, a refusal to be consoled. Benjamin’s morose silence is the farm’s unspoken verdict on the revolution: things are not better, they are not worse, they are simply broken, and the only creature who understood that has been carted away.
ex_en: Benjamin’s morose withdrawal after Boxer’s death was the only honest epitaph the farm would ever give its most loyal worker.
ex_zh: Morose as a winter sky without stars, Benjamin carried his grief in the corners of his mouth, never speaking it, never letting it leave.

def_en: reserved or uncommunicative in speech; saying very little
def_zh: taciturn nature, remain taciturn, taciturn companion
quote_en: "...since Boxer's death, more morose and taciturn than ever." – The narrator pairing Benjamin’s deepened silence with his gloom (Chapter 10)
quote_zh: ‘Taciturn’ describes not just silence but a constitutional reluctance to speak. The word, from the Latin *tacitus* (silent), suggests a temperament rather than a passing mood, and Benjamin has always been taciturn. Yet Boxer’s death intensifies this trait; where once Benjamin’s silence seemed wise, it now feels like a wound. A taciturn person withholds words as a form of self‑protection, but on Animal Farm, taciturnity is also a survival strategy: Benjamin knows that to speak too much is to risk the dogs. The adjective elevates his silence into a philosophical stance—a refusal to participate in the noise of propaganda—but it also reveals his tragic limitation. Taciturn wisdom cannot save anyone who cannot hear it.
ex_en: Benjamin’s taciturn loyalty to Boxer was a friendship built not on words but on Sundays in the paddock, grazing side by side without needing to speak.
ex_zh: Taciturn as the stones in the quarry, Benjamin kept his thoughts inside until they calcified into a hardness nothing could break—not even the whine of the van pulling away.

def_en: impossible to solve; having no solution
def_zh: insoluble problem, insoluble mystery, find insoluble
quote_en: "The animals found the problem insoluble; in any case, they had little time for speculating on such things now." – The narrator describing the animals’ inability to compare their present lives with the pre‑Rebellion past (Chapter 10)
quote_zh: The adjective ‘insoluble’ is epistemological defeat in a single word. The animals cannot determine whether life is better or worse because they lack the tools—memory, literacy, access to unaltered records—to make the comparison. The word, from the Latin *solvere* (to loosen, to dissolve), implies that the problem is not merely difficult but fundamentally resistant to resolution, a knot that cannot be untied. Squealer’s lists of figures dissolve memory, and the animals’ own minds are too tired to hold onto the past. By declaring the problem insoluble, they surrender the right to judge their own experience, and this surrender is exactly what the pigs require. The insoluble question keeps the animals passive, too bewildered to rebel.
ex_en: The animals abandoned the question of whether they were better off as insoluble, and in doing so they abandoned the only tool that might have saved them.
ex_zh: The problem hung in the barn like a riddle written in disappearing ink, the letters fading even as the animals squinted at them with their tired, honest eyes.

def_en: seeking or intended to undermine an established system or institution
def_zh: subversive ideas, subversive activity, subversive element
quote_en: "For a long time there had been rumours — circulated, he had reason to think, by some malignant enemy — that there was something subversive and even revolutionary in the outlook of himself and his colleagues." – Napoleon addressing the human farmers at the final banquet, denying any revolutionary intent (Chapter 10)
quote_zh: The adjective ‘subversive’ is the word Napoleon must publicly disown to complete his transformation. For years, Animalism was the most subversive idea in the county; now the Leader presents it as a misunderstanding spread by malignant enemies. The word, from the Latin *subvertere* (to turn from below, to overturn), describes exactly what the Rebellion was supposed to do. By denying that he or his colleagues are subversive, Napoleon announces that the revolution is over, and the pigs have become the very thing they overthrew. The pairing of ‘subversive and even revolutionary’ is a rhetorical masterstroke: it lumps the farm’s founding ideals into a category of threats that must be suppressed, and the humans applaud.
ex_en: The word ‘subversive’ that Napoleon denied at the banquet had once been the proudest badge of Animal Farm, now tossed aside like a dirty flag.
ex_zh: Subversive was a seed the pigs had planted themselves, but they pulled it up by the roots at the banquet table, handing the bare earth to the farmers as a gift.

def_en: to forcibly put an end to; to prevent the dissemination of
def_zh: suppress a custom, suppress dissent, brutally suppress
quote_en: "Hitherto the animals on the farm had had a rather foolish custom of addressing one another as 'Comrade.' This was to be suppressed." – Napoleon announcing the abolition of the word ‘Comrade’ at the final banquet (Chapter 10)
quote_zh: The verb ‘suppressed’ is the final, definitive act of linguistic policing. To suppress a custom is not merely to discourage it but to erase it by force, to remove it from the permitted repertoire of speech. The word ‘Comrade’ was the glue of the revolution, the title that declared every animal equal. By declaring it a ‘foolish custom’ and ordering it suppressed, Napoleon completes the linguistic counter‑revolution: the animals may no longer speak as though they were equal because they are no longer equal. The passive construction—‘was to be suppressed’—absolves Napoleon of direct agency, but the threat of the dogs makes the order unanswerable. Language itself is now under the whip.
ex_en: When the word ‘Comrade’ was suppressed, the animals lost their last shared possession, a syllable that had once meant everyone.
ex_zh: The pigs suppressed the word ‘Comrade’ as easily as blowing out a candle, and the darkness that followed was filled with the sound of animals who no longer knew what to call each other.

def_en: the remnants of a liquid left in a container, together with any sediment; the least valuable part
def_zh: empty to the dregs, drink to the dregs, dregs of society
quote_en: "There was the same hearty cheering as before, and the mugs were emptied to the dregs." – The narrator observing the farmers and pigs draining their beer at the final toast (Chapter 10)
quote_zh: The image of the mugs emptied ‘to the dregs’ is the perfect closing symbol for the revolution. Dregs are the bitter sediment at the bottom of a drink, the residue that nobody wants but that must be swallowed by those who drink to the final drop. The pigs and humans drain their mugs in a toast to prosperity, but the animals outside, watching through the window, have already been given the dregs of the revolution: hard labour, short rations, and a whip they recognise. The word’s earthy, visceral quality—the grit on the tongue, the sludge in the glass—grounds the abstract betrayal in physical sensation. The revolution has been drunk dry, and only the dregs remain.
ex_en: The pigs and men drank the beer to the dregs, but it was the animals outside who had spent years swallowing the bitter bottom of the cup.
ex_zh: The dregs of the revolution settled in the animals’ water bowls, a thin, brown film that tasted of promises too stale to swallow.

def_en: famous and respected within a particular sphere; prominent
def_zh: eminent pigs, eminent position, most eminent
quote_en: "There, round the long table, sat half a dozen farmers and half a dozen of the more eminent pigs" – The narrator describing the seating arrangement at the banquet that ends the novel (Chapter 10)
quote_zh: ‘Eminent’ confers an aura of distinction and worth upon the pigs who sit among humans as equals. The word, from the Latin *eminere* (to stand out, to project), implies that these pigs have risen above the common herd—not by force, but by merit. Yet the reader knows that their eminence is built on lies, theft, and murder. The adjective naturalises the pigs’ status, making their presence at the table seem not a betrayal of Animalism but a logical culmination of their superiority. The other animals, who are not eminent, remain outside, pressing their faces to the glass. The word draws a final, unbreachable line between the rulers and the ruled, and the reader understands that eminence, on this farm, has always meant proximity to the whip.
ex_en: The eminent pigs who sat at the table were the same animals who had once drunk the milk meant for everyone and called it medicine.
ex_zh: Eminent as lords in a painting, the pigs settled into their chairs, their snouts lifting with an ease that made the animals outside feel the cold glass against their noses.

def_en: necessary for someone as a duty or responsibility
def_zh: incumbent upon, feel incumbent, incumbent duty
quote_en: "In a moment, he said, he would ask the present company to drink a toast. But before doing so, there were a few words that he felt it incumbent upon him to say." – Mr. Pilkington preparing to praise the pigs’ regime before the toast (Chapter 10)
quote_zh: The phrase ‘incumbent upon him’ is the verbal equivalent of a bow and a smirk. Pilkington presents his coming speech of self‑congratulation as a solemn duty, a moral obligation he must discharge. The word, from the Latin *incumbere* (to lie upon, to press upon), suggests a weight of responsibility that Pilkington pretends to bear. In reality, his words are a business toast, a celebration of the low rations and long working hours he intends to copy on his own farm. Orwell uses the polite, formal register of ‘incumbent’ to expose the oily hypocrisy of a man who dresses exploitation in the language of gentlemanly obligation. The words that are ‘incumbent’ upon him are nothing but a deal dressed in dinner‑jacket flattery.
ex_en: The duty Pilkington felt incumbent upon him was to congratulate the pigs on starving their animals more efficiently than any human farmer had managed.
ex_zh: Incumbent upon every pig that night was the lie they had to swallow with their beer, and they swallowed it as smoothly as cream.

def_en: a feeling of doubt or apprehension about the outcome of something
def_zh: a certain misgiving, deep misgiving, look with misgiving
quote_en: "There had been a time — not that he, or any of the present company, had shared such sentiments — but there had been a time when the respected proprietors of Animal Farm had been regarded, he would not say with hostility, but perhaps with a certain measure of misgiving, by their human neighbours." – Mr. Pilkington delicately admitting past doubts before dismissing them entirely (Chapter 10)
quote_zh: ‘Misgiving’ is the gentlest possible word for the fear and hatred that once existed between humans and Animal Farm. It suggests a mild unease, a slight hesitation, a ‘certain measure’ of doubt—language that carefully shrinks the past into something manageable and forgettable. The word’s softness is strategic: by characterizing the old hostility as mere ‘misgiving’, Pilkington makes reconciliation sound easy, a simple clearing of the air. The humans had trembled at ‘Beasts of England’; they had tried to recapture the farm by force—but now it is all reduced to a quaint misunderstanding. The word sanitises the conflict so thoroughly that the animals outside the window can hardly connect it to the battles they fought.
ex_en: The misgiving Pilkington mentioned so delicately had once been a war, and the dead sheep under the hawthorn were not misgivings but corpses.
ex_zh: Misgiving was a small, careful word that Pilkington placed on the table like a toothpick, while the bones of the revolution lay buried in the orchard.
